name: Al-Badi
title: Al-Badi: The Originator without precedent
meta_description: Al-Badi, the Originator without precedent, built from the Qur'an and classical tafsir. The One who invents the heavens and the earth with no prior model and no partner, who only says Be and it is, and the same root that gives us the word for novelty itself. Qur'an verified.
keywords: Al-Badi, Al-Badee, The Originator, The Incomparable Originator, badi al-samawati wal-ard, names of Allah, 99 names, kun fa-yakun, Surah Al-Baqarah 2:117, Surah Al-Anam 6:101, Asma ul Husna, Buruja
source_note: Grounded in the Qur'an (Sahih International, verified via quran.ai) and classical tafsir (Ibn Kathir, including his citation of Ibn Jarir al-Tabari, Tafsir as-Sa'di, al-Tafsir al-Muyassar, and al-Jalalayn), in the voice of Buruja.
lede: Stand outside on a clear night and let the sky do what it was made to do to you. Not a single star up there was copied from a blueprint. No two snowflakes that ever fell were the same. No two faces in the billions of people alive carry the exact same arrangement, no two fingerprints, no two voices, no two grains of sand under a microscope. Creation is not a factory stamping out copies. It is an endless, unrepeating act of invention, and somebody is doing the inventing.

Al-Badi, the Originator without precedent. The One who brings things into being on no prior model, from no earlier example, with no partner to help and no template to follow, because He is the First Maker and there was nothing before Him to imitate. The Qur'an names Him this at the two places where it answers the deepest confusion a person can have about God: that He must have come from somewhere, that He must be like the things we know. Al-Badi is the name that says no. He is the source, not a product. He invents; He is not invented.

sections.h2: The name, and the verse it lives in

ayah.arabic: بَدِيعُ السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضِ ۖ وَإِذَا قَضَىٰ أَمْرًا فَإِنَّمَا يَقُولُ لَهُ كُن فَيَكُونُ
ayah.text: Originator of the heavens and the earth. When He decrees a matter, He only says to it, "Be," and it is.

paras: Start with the word. Badi comes from three Arabic letters, ba, dal, ayn, the root of ibda', to bring something into being that has no prior likeness. Ibn Kathir, explaining this very verse, says it means the Creator of the heavens and the earth on no earlier model (ala ghayr mithal sabaq), and he notes something striking about the language: this is the same root that gives the Arabic word bid'ah, an innovation, a thing newly introduced that had no precedent before it. The name is built from the very idea of the unprecedented.

He then carries the words of the early scholar Ibn Jarir al-Tabari, and calls his explanation excellent and sound. Badi, al-Tabari says, is grammatically a form that means al-mubdi, the Originator: the One who brings into being and produces what no one preceded Him in producing the like of. Ibn Kathir relays al-Suddi's gloss in the same breath: He originated them, having created nothing at all before them that they could be patterned on. Sit with that last phrase. There was no earlier thing for the heavens to be copied from. They are the first of their kind, and so is everything in them.

And look at how the verse finishes, because the second half is the engine of the first. When He decrees a matter, He only says to it, Be, and it is. Ibn Kathir explains that this shows the perfection of His power and the greatness of His authority: when He wills a thing into existence, He says to it kun, once, and it comes to be exactly as He intended. The Originator needs no workshop, no materials, no rehearsal. The distance between His will and a brand new universe is a single word.

We need this name precisely because we have no real experience of what it describes. Everything a human being has ever made was made from something, and modelled on something. The first chair imitated a rock you could sit on. Every poem is built from a language someone else handed you, every invention from parts that already existed, every child from two parents who came before. We do not originate. At our most creative, we rearrange. We are, all of us, working from a precedent we did not set.

Al-Badi is the breaking of that entire pattern. Al-Sa'di, commenting on the name, writes that Allah is the Creator of the heavens and the earth who perfected them and made them beautiful on no prior model. Not assembled from older stock, not improved from a first draft, not patterned on a rival's design, because there was no older stock, no first draft, no rival. When the Qur'an wants you to grasp this, it does not hand you a bigger version of human making. It hands you a different category of act altogether: origination from nothing, by a Maker who answers to no example.

This is also why the name so often appears in the Qur'an as Originator of the heavens and the earth, the two largest things you can point to. It is as if you are told: take the most enormous, most intricate realities you know, the sky over your head and the ground under your feet, and now understand that these were invented, brought out of sheer nothing, by One who had nothing to copy. If He can originate that, the small impossible thing you are carrying is not too new for Him to make.

sections.h2: The name that refuses Him a partner

ayah.arabic: بَدِيعُ السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضِ ۖ أَنَّىٰ يَكُونُ لَهُ وَلَدٌ وَلَمْ تَكُن لَّهُ صَاحِبَةٌ ۖ وَخَلَقَ كُلَّ شَيْءٍ ۖ وَهُوَ بِكُلِّ شَيْءٍ عَلِيمٌ
ayah.text: [He is] Originator of the heavens and the earth. How could He have a son when He does not have a companion [i.e., wife] and He created all things? And He is, of all things, Knowing.

paras: The second place the Qur'an names Him Al-Badi puts the name to work, and watch what it does. Originator of the heavens and the earth: how could He have a son when He has no companion, and He created all things? The name itself is the argument. Ibn Kathir spells it out: a child only comes from two beings of the same kind, joined together, and Allah is the One whom nothing in creation resembles or matches, because He is the Creator of every single thing. The One who originates everything cannot Himself be a thing that was originated. He stands outside the whole system of causes that He invented.

Al-Sa'di draws the same line with care. Allah, he says, is the Master, the Self-Sufficient, who has no consort and no equal, rich beyond all His creation while every creature is utterly poor before Him; and a child must be of the same genus as its parent, yet nothing among the created things resembles Allah in any respect whatsoever. To call Him Al-Badi is to say He has no precedent above Him and no partner beside Him. He did not emerge from anything, so He is owed by everything.

Notice what kind of comfort this is. People reach for a God who is like them, a bigger version of a human father, because the truly unlike is hard to hold. But the verse insists on His difference on purpose, and the difference is the good news. A God who needed a partner would be a God with a need, and a God with a need cannot be wholly relied on. Al-Badi has no need at the root of Him. He is not one more link in a chain; He is the One who made the chain, and that is exactly why He can hold it.

Once you carry this name, you start to read the world differently. The mufassirun ground Al-Badi in the verse, and the verse points you straight at the heavens and the earth and tells you to look. So look. The thing that should astonish you about creation is not only its size or its order, but its refusal to repeat itself. The same Maker who hung the galaxies also designed the structure of a single leaf, the spiral inside a shell, the particular blue at the edge of a particular morning, and He did not run out of new forms anywhere.

Al-Sa'di, commenting on Al-Badi at Surah Al-Anam, reaches for exactly this: Allah originated the heavens and the earth in the finest of creation, in order and in splendour, the like of which the minds of people of intellect could not even conceive, and He has no partner in any of it. That phrase, the minds of the intelligent could not conceive its like, is worth holding. The cleverest person who ever lived could not have invented the design of an eye from scratch, could not have dreamed up the system that turns sunlight into a forest. Al-Badi did, and then varied it past counting.

We might reflect, gently and as our own contemplation rather than as a ruling, that this is why genuine wonder in front of nature is a form of worship. When a believer is stopped in their tracks by something they have never seen before, a sky they have no words for, they are meeting a trace of Al-Badi: the inventiveness that never once reached for a copy. _Note: this reflection on the novelty woven through creation is contemplation (tadabbur) drawn from how al-Sa'di and Ibn Kathir gloss origination on no prior model, and it incorporates reasoning beyond the fetched text. It is offered as reflection and does not constitute a scholarly ruling or opinion from quran.ai, quran.com, or quran.foundation._

sections.h2: Where it stands among the names of making
paras: Allah names Himself the Maker in more than one way, and the differences are not decoration; each name opens a slightly different window. Al-Khaliq is the One who creates and measures out, who determines a thing and proportions it. Al-Bari is the One who brings forth and separates a creation into distinct existence, free of fault. Al-Musawwir is the One who fashions the forms, who gives each created thing its particular shape and feature. These names dwell on the act and the craft of bringing into being.

Al-Badi sits slightly apart, and it is worth feeling where. Its weight is on the unprecedented: not only that He makes, and not only how finely He makes, but that He makes with no prior example anywhere in front of Him. Ibn Kathir, drawing on al-Tabari, fixed the meaning at exactly this point, the One who originates what no one came before Him in originating. So when you call Allah Al-Khaliq you are praising the Creator; when you call Him Al-Bari you are praising the flawless bringing forth; when you call Him Al-Musawwir you are praising the shaping of the form; and when you call Him Al-Badi you are praising the sheer originality of it, that none of it was copied, that He is the First Maker who needed no maker before Him.

Hold the family of names together and the picture completes. He determines (Al-Khaliq), He brings forth without flaw (Al-Bari), He fashions every form (Al-Musawwir), and the whole project rests on an origination that had no precedent at all (Al-Badi). The God you worship is not refining someone else's work. He began it, all of it, from nothing, and signed every piece with something that had never existed before.

sections.h2: What this name asks of you
paras: A name of Allah is never only information about Him. It is meant to move something in you, and Al-Badi moves at least three things.

First, it kills despair at situations that feel new and impossible. Part of what makes a hardship crush us is the sense that there is no precedent for it, no map, no one who has been exactly here, so there is no way out. But the One you are asking is the Originator who specialises in what has no precedent. He is not limited to recycling old solutions. He can open a door where there has never been a door, make a way out of a corner that has always been sealed, begin a thing in your life that nothing in your past would predict. The whole creation is proof that He does not need an existing model to make something real.

Second, it teaches you how to ask. The youths of the cave, cornered with no plan and no template for survival, did not ask for the old normal back. They turned to their Lord and said, grant us from Yourself mercy and prepare for us from our affair right guidance. That is how you pray to Al-Badi: not only fix the familiar, but originate for me a way I cannot see, a guidance I could not have invented. You are speaking to the One for whom a brand new outcome is no harder than the word Be.

Third, it humbles your sense of your own making. Everything you are proud of building, you built from gifts you were given, in a world you did not invent, with a mind handed to you finished. Al-Badi reminds you that true origination belongs to Allah alone, and the most a creature ever does is arrange the wonders that were already laid out. That is not meant to deflate you. It is meant to turn your creativity into gratitude, and to keep the credit flowing back to the only One who ever made something from nothing.

sections.h2: The First Maker, who needs no precedent

ayah.arabic: رَبَّنَا آتِنَا مِن لَّدُنكَ رَحْمَةً وَهَيِّئْ لَنَا مِنْ أَمْرِنَا رَشَدًا
ayah.text: Our Lord, grant us from Yourself mercy and prepare for us from our affair right guidance.

paras: Step back and let the whole name settle. Before there was anything, there was Allah, and there was no second thing for Him to resemble or to lean on. When He brought the heavens and the earth out of nothing, He drew on no earlier draft, no rival design, no partner's hand. Al-Sa'di's words were that He made them in the finest order and splendour, the like of which the brightest minds could not conceive, and He had no associate in any of it. Ibn Kathir's words were that He originated what no one preceded Him in originating, and that He says to a thing Be, and it is.

That is the God you call when you have run out of precedent. The youths of the cave prayed exactly there, at the edge of a situation no one had charted: prepare for us from our affair right guidance. They were asking the Originator to bring into being a way that did not yet exist, and He did. So can you. The same inventiveness that scattered the unrepeating stars, that made no two mornings alike, is turned toward the servant who asks Him for a beginning. He has never once needed a model to make something new, and He does not need one now, for you.

To know Al-Badi is to stop asking only for the return of the old and start trusting the One who can originate the unforeseen. He is the First Maker. Nothing came before Him, and nothing constrains Him to the past. The new mercy you cannot picture is, to the Originator of the heavens and the earth, a single word away.

dua.arabic: رَبَّنَا آتِنَا مِن لَّدُنكَ رَحْمَةً وَهَيِّئْ لَنَا مِنْ أَمْرِنَا رَشَدًا
dua.translit: Rabbana atina min ladunka rahmatan wa hayyi lana min amrina rashada
dua.en: Our Lord, grant us from Yourself mercy and prepare for us from our affair right guidance.

lessons.bold: Refuse despair at what feels unprecedented.
lessons.text: Al-Badi originates what has no prior model. He can open a door where there was never a door. Ibn Kathir explains the name as the One who brings into being what no one came before Him in bringing into being, on no earlier example.

lessons.bold: Ask for a way you cannot yet see.
lessons.text: The youths of the cave, cornered with no plan, prayed: prepare for us from our affair right guidance (18:10). Pray to the Originator not only to fix the familiar, but to begin for you what has never existed.

lessons.bold: Let creation stop you in your tracks.
lessons.text: No two faces, no two snowflakes, no two mornings are the same. Al-Sa'di says He made the heavens and earth in a splendour the brightest minds could not conceive. Genuine wonder at the unrepeating is a way of meeting Al-Badi.

lessons.bold: Worship the One who has no partner.
lessons.text: At 6:101 the name itself answers those who give Allah a son or an equal: the Originator of all things cannot be a thing that was originated. As al-Sa'di notes, nothing among the created resembles Him in any respect.

lessons.bold: Return the credit for your own making.
lessons.text: Everything you build, you build from gifts you were given in a world you did not invent. True origination belongs to Allah alone. Let that turn your creativity into gratitude rather than pride.

faq.q: What does the name Al-Badi mean?
faq.a: Al-Badi (بديع) means the Originator without precedent, from the root b-d-' which carries the sense of bringing into being something with no prior likeness. Commenting on Surah Al-Baqarah 2:117, Ibn Kathir explains it as the Creator of the heavens and the earth on no earlier model (ala ghayr mithal sabaq), and notes it is the same root that gives the Arabic word bid'ah, an innovation with no precedent. Quoting Ibn Jarir al-Tabari, he glosses it as al-mubdi, the One who originates what no one preceded Him in originating. It is not merely that Allah makes, but that He makes from no example at all.

faq.q: Where does the name Al-Badi appear in the Qur'an?
faq.a: The word badi describing Allah appears in the Qur'an in two places, both in the same phrase: badi al-samawati wal-ard, Originator of the heavens and the earth, at Al-Baqarah 2:117 and Al-Anam 6:101. In both it is in the construct form (Originator of...) rather than the standalone definite name al-Badi, but the classical scholars include it among the names of Allah on the basis of these verses and the Names tradition. Buruja grounds it exactly here, in the Qur'anic phrase, verified word for word.

faq.q: How is Al-Badi different from Al-Khaliq, Al-Bari, and Al-Musawwir?
faq.a: All four are names of Allah as Maker, but each opens a different window. Al-Khaliq is the One who creates and measures out; Al-Bari is the One who brings forth into distinct existence without flaw; Al-Musawwir is the One who fashions each form. Al-Badi puts its weight on the unprecedented: that He makes with no prior example before Him. As Ibn Kathir (drawing on al-Tabari) fixes it, Al-Badi is the One who originates what no one came before Him in originating. So Al-Badi emphasises the sheer originality of creation, that none of it was copied and He is the First Maker who needed no maker.

faq.q: How does Al-Badi connect to 'Be, and it is' (kun fa-yakun)?
faq.a: In Al-Baqarah 2:117 the name is immediately followed by: When He decrees a matter, He only says to it, Be, and it is. Ibn Kathir explains that this shows the perfection of Allah's power: when He wills a thing into existence, He says kun, once, and it comes to be exactly as He intended. The two halves belong together. Al-Badi originates without any prior model, and the way He originates is by sheer command. There is no workshop and no materials between His will and a brand new reality, only the word Be.

closing_heading: Why this name stays with us
closing: We carry a quiet assumption that everything has to come from something, that nothing truly new is possible, that the future can only be a rearrangement of the past. Al-Badi is the Qur'an's answer to that, not as a comforting idea but as a name of God. He is the Originator of the heavens and the earth who drew on no earlier draft and no partner's hand, the One in whose vocabulary a brand new universe is a single word, Be. The scholars fix the meaning at exactly this point: the One who brings into being what no one preceded Him in bringing into being, in a splendour the brightest minds could not conceive. The same inventiveness that made no two stars and no two mornings alike is turned, right now, toward you. The new way you cannot picture is not too new for Him to make.

O Allah, Al-Badi, Originator of the heavens and the earth, You brought all of this out of nothing on no prior model and with no partner beside You, and You say to a thing Be, and it is. Originate for us what we cannot see and could never invent: open doors where there have never been doors, begin in our lives the good that nothing in our past would promise, and turn our wonder at Your unrepeating creation into worship of You alone. Rabbana atina min ladunka rahmatan wa hayyi lana min amrina rashada.

slides:
Al-Badi.

The Originator without precedent.

Not a single star was copied from a blueprint. No two snowflakes, no two faces, no two mornings are the same.

Creation is not a factory stamping out copies. It is an endless act of invention. And Someone is doing the inventing.

The Qur'an names Him this twice, in the same phrase:

'Originator of the heavens and the earth...' (2:117, 6:101)

Ibn Kathir: it means the Creator of them on **no prior model.** The same Arabic root gives the word for an innovation that had no precedent.

We never truly originate.

Every poem uses a language someone handed us. Every invention reuses parts that already existed. At our most creative, we only **rearrange.**

Al-Badi is the breaking of that entire pattern: making from nothing, with no example to copy.

Look at how 2:117 finishes:

'...When He decrees a matter, He only says to it, **Be, and it is.**'

No workshop. No materials. No rehearsal. The distance between His will and a brand new universe is a single word.

At 6:101 the name becomes the argument:

'Originator of the heavens and the earth. How could He have a son when He has no companion, and He created all things?'

Al-Sa'di on this name:

He made the heavens and the earth in an order and splendour **the brightest minds could not even conceive,** and He has no partner in any of it.

The cleverest human could not invent an eye from scratch. Al-Badi did, then varied it past counting.

How is this different from Al-Khaliq, Al-Bari, Al-Musawwir?

Those dwell on the act and craft of making.

Al-Badi puts the weight on the **unprecedented:** that He makes with no example anywhere in front of Him. He is the First Maker who needed no maker.

Why it matters when life feels impossible:

What crushes us is the sense that our situation has no precedent, no map, no way out.

But the One you are asking is the Originator who specialises in what has no precedent. He can open a door where there has never been a door.

The youths of the cave, cornered with no plan, did not ask for the old normal back.

They said: 'prepare for us from our affair right guidance.' (18:10)

That is how you pray to Al-Badi. Not only fix the familiar. Originate for me a way I cannot see. 🤍
social.caption:
Al-Badi, the Originator without precedent.

Stand under a clear sky tonight. Not one star was copied from a blueprint. No two snowflakes, no two faces, no two fingerprints, no two mornings are ever the same. Creation is not a factory stamping out copies. It is an endless act of invention, and Someone is doing the inventing.

The Qur'an names Allah this in two places, both in the same phrase: 'Originator of the heavens and the earth' (2:117, 6:101). Grounded in classical tafsir (Ibn Kathir, including his citation of Ibn Jarir al-Tabari, and al-Sa'di), the name means the One who brings the heavens and the earth into being on no prior model, from no earlier example, with no partner to help. It is the same Arabic root that gives the word for an innovation that had no precedent before it.

We never truly originate. Every poem uses a language we were handed; every invention reuses parts that already existed; at our most creative, we only rearrange. Al-Badi breaks that pattern entirely: making from nothing, by a Maker who answers to no example. And look how 2:117 finishes: 'When He decrees a matter, He only says to it, Be, and it is.' The distance between His will and a brand new universe is a single word.

Here is why it steadies you. What crushes us in hardship is the feeling that our situation is unprecedented, that there is no way out. But the One you are asking is the Originator who specialises in what has no precedent. The youths of the cave, cornered with no plan, did not ask for the old normal back; they prayed, 'prepare for us from our affair right guidance' (18:10).

So ask Him for a way you cannot yet see. 🤍

email.subject: The One who needs no blueprint
email.alt_subject: When your situation has no precedent
email.preview: Al-Badi, the Originator without precedent. No two snowflakes, no two mornings, and a God who makes the never-before from nothing.
email.body:
Assalamu alaikum,

Stand outside on a clear night and let the sky do what it was made to do. Not a single star up there was copied from a blueprint. No two snowflakes that ever fell were the same. No two faces, no two fingerprints, no two voices, no two grains of sand under a microscope. Creation is not a factory stamping out copies. It is an endless, unrepeating act of invention, and Someone is doing the inventing.

One of the names of Allah is exactly this: Al-Badi, the Originator without precedent.

The Qur'an names Him this in two places, both in the same phrase: 'Originator of the heavens and the earth' (Al-Baqarah 2:117 and Al-Anam 6:101). Commenting on it, Ibn Kathir explains that it means the Creator of the heavens and the earth on no earlier model, and he notes that it is the same Arabic root that gives the word for an innovation that had no precedent before it. Quoting the early scholar Ibn Jarir al-Tabari, he glosses the name as the One who brings into being and produces what no one preceded Him in producing the like of. There was simply no earlier thing for creation to be copied from.

We need this name because we have no real experience of what it describes. Everything a human being has ever made was made from something, and modelled on something. Every poem is built from a language someone handed us; every invention from parts that already existed. We do not originate. At our most creative, we rearrange. Al-Badi is the breaking of that entire pattern: making from nothing, by a Maker who answers to no example. And look how the verse finishes: 'When He decrees a matter, He only says to it, Be, and it is.' There is no workshop and no materials between His will and a brand new reality, only the word Be.

Al-Sa'di, commenting on the name, says that Allah made the heavens and the earth in the finest order and splendour, the like of which the minds of people of intellect could not even conceive, and that He has no partner in any of it. The cleverest person who ever lived could not have invented the design of an eye from scratch. Al-Badi did, and then varied it past counting.

Here is why this name steadies you. Part of what makes a hardship crush us is the sense that there is no precedent for it, no map, no way out. But the One you are asking is the Originator who specialises in what has no precedent. He is not limited to recycling old solutions. He can open a door where there has never been a door, and begin a thing in your life that nothing in your past would predict. When the youths of the cave were cornered with no plan, they did not ask for the old normal back. They prayed, 'Our Lord, grant us from Yourself mercy and prepare for us from our affair right guidance' (Al-Kahf 18:10). They were asking the Originator to bring into being a way that did not yet exist, and He did.

So pray like that. Ask Him not only to fix the familiar, but to originate for you a way you cannot see. The same inventiveness that scattered the unrepeating stars is turned toward the servant who asks Him for a beginning.

Ya Badi, originate for us what we cannot picture, and prepare for us from our affair right guidance.

If you would like the full reflection, we have written it here:
